answer:
We assume the premises true and the conclusion false. For ~P to be false, P must be true.  
From ~(~Q ∨ R) true, we get ~Q false and R false, so Q true and R false.  
With P true, Q true, R false, the first premise P ⊃ (Q ∨ R) becomes true ⊃ true, which is true.  
The second premise (Q · S) ⊃ ~P becomes (true · S) ⊃ false, which requires S false to be true.  
All premises can be true with P true, Q true, R false, S false, making the conclusion false, so the argument is invalid.  

\boxed{C}
